papers.title: Hypervelocity stars from young stellar clusters in the Galactic Centre
papers.author_names: Giacomo Fragione Roberto Capuzzo-Dolcetta Pavel Kroupa

abstract.text: The enormous velocities of the so called hypervelocity stars (HVSs) derive, likely, from close interactions with massive black holes, binary stars encounters or supernova explosions. In this paper, we investigate the origin of hypervelocity stars as consequence of the close interaction between the Milky Way central massive black hole and a passing-by young stellar cluster. We found that both single and binary HVSs may be generated in a burst-like event, as the cluster passes near the orbital pericentre. High velocity stars will move close to the initial cluster orbital plane and in the direction of the cluster orbital motion at the pericentre. The binary fraction of these HVS jets depends on the primordial binary fraction in the young cluster. The level of initial mass segregation determines the value of the average mass of the ejected stars. Some binary stars will merge, continuing their travel across and out of the Galaxy as blue stragglers.

brief.tldr: [research paper] This paper models the formation of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) via dynamical interactions between a young stellar cluster (YSC) and the Milky Way's central supermassive black hole (SgrA*), showing that both single and binary HVSs can be ejected in burst-like events during pericentre passage. The ejection velocity and binary fraction depend on the cluster's initial binary fraction and mass segregation, with some binaries merging into blue stragglers during ejection. Results demonstrate that YSC-BH interactions provide a viable, physically motivated mechanism for producing high-velocity stellar ejecta with distinct kinematic signatures.

source_fields.title: Hypervelocity stars from young stellar clusters in the Galactic Centre

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Method: The paper proposes an $N$-body simulation method to study the evolution of young star clusters interacting with SgrA* via direct numerical integration, using initial conditions generated by McLuster with varying binary fractions and mass segregation levels based on physical prescriptions for cluster formation and binary statistics. Results: Increasing the initial binary fraction of the cluster leads to more ejected binary hypervelocity stars (HVSs) and a decreasing fraction of single HVSs, with single HVSs arising from binary disruption, single-star ejection, and binary splitting, while binary HVSs exclusively result from binary disruption during the cluster's close passage to the supermassive black hole. Conclusions: This study concludes that hyper-velocity stars are produced during close encounters of star clusters with the Milky Way's central supermassive black hole, with their properties—such as velocity, binary fraction, mass, and anisotropic distribution—strongly influenced by initial cluster conditions and the galactic environment, offering insights into both cluster dynamics and the structure of the galactic center. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel approach to identify and characterize hypervelocity stars by leveraging Gaia's precise astrometric data to resolve their three-dimensional orbits and kinematics, thereby addressing the critical problem of lacking tangential velocity measurements in existing MMT surveys and enabling a more accurate determination of their origins and the Galactic potential's structure.

abstract: The enormous velocities of the so called hypervelocity stars (HVSs) derive, likely, from close interactions with massive black holes, binary stars encounters or supernova explosions.
In this paper, we investigate the origin of hypervelocity stars as consequence of the close interaction between the Milky Way central massive black hole and a passing-by young stellar cluster.
High velocity stars will move close to the initial cluster orbital plane and in the direction of the cluster orbital motion at the pericentre.
The level of initial mass segregation determines the value of the average mass of the ejected stars.
Some binary stars will merge, continuing their travel across and out of the Galaxy as blue stragglers.
title: Hypervelocity stars from young stellar clusters in the Galactic Centre

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical simulation paper modeling HVS formation via cluster-SMBH interactions. No observational data or object-level catalog presented.

papers.title: Transits Probabilities Around Hypervelocity and Runaway Stars

abstract.text: In the blooming field of exoplanetary science, NASA's Kepler Space Telescope has revolutionized our understanding of exoplanets. Kepler's very precise and long-duration photometry is ideal for detecting planetary transits around Sun-like stars. The forthcoming Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS) is expected to continue Kepler's legacy. Along with transits, the Doppler technique remains an invaluable tool for discovering planets. The next generation of spectrographs, such as G-CLEF, promises precision radial velocity measurements. In this paper, we explore the possibility of detecting planets around hypervelocity and runaway stars, which should host a very compact system as consequence of their turbulent origin. We find that the probability of a multi-planetary transit is $10^{-3}\\\\lesssim P\\\\lesssim 10^{-1}$. We therefore need to observe $\\\\sim 10-1000$ high-velocity stars to spot a transit. However, even if transits are rare around runaway and hypervelocity stars, the chances of detecting such planets using radial velocity surveys is high. We predict that the European Gaia satellite, along with TESS and the new-generation spectrographs G-CLEF and ESPRESSO, will spot planetary systems orbiting high-velocity stars.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper calculates the transit probability for planets orbiting hypervelocity and runaway stars, finding that multi-planet transit likelihood lies between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-1}$, implying that tens to thousands of such stars must be observed to detect a transit. Despite low transit rates, radial velocity surveys with Gaia, TESS, G-CLEF, and ESPRESSO are predicted to efficiently detect planetary systems around these high-velocity stars due to their strong Doppler signatures, offering insights into planetary survival in extreme dynamical environments.

source_fields.title: Transits Probabilities Around Hypervelocity and Runaway Stars

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Method: The paper proposes using the CORBITS algorithm to numerically compute the combined geometric transit probability for multi-planet systems by accounting for the complex orbital interactions and solid angles swept by multiple planets, overcoming the lack of analytical solutions in such systems. Conclusions: The likelihood of detecting exoplanets around high-velocity stars via transit or Doppler methods depends on stellar mass, planetary architecture, and observational capabilities, with transits being more probable around low-mass stars due to higher signal-to-noise ratios and Doppler detections favored by compact systems around low-mass stars. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel approach to detecting exoplanets around high-velocity stars by combining Doppler spectroscopy and transit methods, addressing the lack of systematic planet searches in this unique stellar population that may harbor distinct planetary system architectures. Radial Velocities: The radial velocity technique detects exoplanets by measuring the periodic Doppler shifts in a star's spectrum caused by planetary orbital motion, with detection sensitivity depending on the stellar velocity amplitude, measurement precision, observation duration, and signal-to-noise ratio. Geometrical transits: The geometrical transit probabilities for exoplanets depend on stellar mass, orbital eccentricity, inclination, and semi-major axis, with massive stars and high eccentricities increasing transit likelihood, though detection is limited by flux decrement and the requirement for orbital alignment. Observing real transits: A transit can only be detected if it occurs within the observing baseline, has a sufficient signal-to-noise ratio due to the planet's size and distance, and meets photometric precision requirements, even if its geometric probability is high.

abstract: Kepler's very precise and long-duration photometry is ideal for detecting planetary transits around Sun-like stars.
In this paper, we explore the possibility of detecting planets around hypervelocity and runaway stars, which should host a very compact system as consequence of their turbulent origin.
We therefore need to observe $\\\\sim 10-1000$ high-velocity stars to spot a transit.
However, even if transits are rare around runaway and hypervelocity stars, the chances of detecting such planets using radial velocity surveys is high.
We predict that the European Gaia satellite, along with TESS and the new-generation spectrographs G-CLEF and ESPRESSO, will spot planetary systems orbiting high-velocity stars.
title: Transits Probabilities Around Hypervelocity and Runaway Stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Theoretical calculation of transit probabilities around HVS/runaway stars. Paper discusses future surveys but presents no observational stellar data or catalog.

papers.title: A comprehensive search for stellar bowshock nebulae in the Milky Way: a catalog of 709 mid-infrared selected candidates
papers.author_names: Henry A. Kobulnicky William T. Chick Danielle P. Schurhammer Julian E. Andrews Matthew S. Povich Stephan A. Munari Grace M. Olivier Rebecca L. Sorber Heather N. Wernke Daniel A. Dale Don M. Dixon

abstract.text: We identify 709 arc-shaped mid-infrared nebula in 24 micron Spitzer Space Telescope or 22 micron Wide Field Infrared Explorer surveys of the Galactic Plane as probable dusty interstellar bowshocks powered by early-type stars. About 20% are visible at 8 microns or shorter mid-infrared wavelengths as well. The vast majority (660) have no previous identification in the literature. These extended infrared sources are strongly concentrated near Galactic mid-Plane with an angular scale height of ~0.6 degrees. All host a symmetrically placed star implicated as the source of a stellar wind sweeping up interstellar material. These are candidate "runaway" stars potentially having high velocities in the reference frame of the local medium. Among the 286 objects with measured proper motions, we find an unambiguous excess having velocity vectors aligned with the infrared morphology --- kinematic evidence that many of these are "runaway" stars with large peculiar motions responsible for the bowshock signature. We discuss a population of "in-situ" bowshocks (103 objects) that face giant HII regions where the relative motions between the star and ISM may be caused by bulk outflows from an overpressured bubble. We also identify 58 objects that face 8 micron bright-rimmed clouds and apparently constitute a sub-class of in-situ bowshocks where the stellar wind interacts with a photo-evaporative flow from an eroding molecular cloud interface (i.e., "PEF bowshocks"). Orientations of the arcuate nebulae exhibit a correlation over small angular scales, indicating that external influences such as HII regions are responsible for producing some bowshock nebulae. However, the vast majority of this sample appear to be isolated (499 objects) from obvious external influences.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Catalog of bowshock nebulae powered by early-type stars, explicitly identifies candidate runaway stars with large peculiar motions and discusses kinematic evidence of high-velocity stars

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Conclusions: This study identifies and characterizes 709 candidate stellar bowshock nebulae, primarily driven by massive runaway stars and their interactions with the interstellar medium, providing a comprehensive resource for investigating stellar winds, runaway stars, and large-scale outflows in the Galaxy. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel analytical and numerical framework to model bowshock nebulae formed by high-velocity runaway stars, addressing the problem of accurately predicting their morphology and kinematics in diverse stellar wind and interstellar medium conditions, particularly for OB stars ejected via dynamical interactions or supernova kicks. Bowshock Candidate Identification: Bowshock candidates were identified through a visual analysis of Spitzer and WISE infrared data by searching for symmetric, arc-shaped nebulae near the Galactic Plane with a prominent star along their axis of symmetry, with final selections made by the senior author to ensure reliability. Properties Of The Bowshock Candidates: This section demonstrates that while prototypical bowshock nebulae exhibit clear arc-shaped morphologies with central stars and proper motion, most candidate bowshocks—especially less-resolved ones—are asymmetric or deviate from ideal shapes, and are distributed across the inner Galaxy with extinction values indicating varying levels of interstellar dust. A Comprehensive Search For Stellar Bowshock Nebulae In The Milky Way: A Catalog Of 709 Mid-Infrared Selected Candidates: This study presents a comprehensive catalog of 709 mid-infrared selected stellar bowshock nebulae candidates in the Milky Way, identified through a systematic search to aid in the study of stellar winds and interstellar medium interactions.

abstract: arc-shaped mid-infrared nebula in 24 micron Spitzer Space Telescope or 22 micron Wide Field Infrared Explorer surveys of the Galactic Plane as probable dusty interstellar bowshocks powered by early-type stars
These are candidate "runaway" stars potentially having high velocities in the reference frame of the local medium.

Among the 286 objects with measured proper motions, we find an unambiguous excess having velocity vectors aligned with the infrared morphology --- kinematic evidence that many of these are "runaway" stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Presents a catalog of 709 mid-infrared selected bowshock nebula candidates, with 660 being new identifications. Includes proper motion measurements for 286 objects and discusses kinematic evidence for runaway stars.
